title: The '<UNK>' Token role
concept: If a character is completely unknown (e.g. emoji in a text-only vocabulary), the tokenizer maps it to a special '<UNK>' (Unknown) token.

exercises.prompt: What does the 'UNK' in the '<UNK>' token stand for?
exercises.explain: The '<UNK>' token stands for 'unknown'. It is used when the system encounters a character or word not present in its vocabulary.

cards.title: A Mystery Symbol!
cards.body: Have you ever tried to read a foreign language and encountered a symbol you have absolutely never seen before? You probably just thought 'unknown symbol' and kept reading. AI tokenizers do the exact same thing!

cards.title: What is the <UNK> Token?
cards.body: The '<UNK>' (Unknown) token is a special placeholder used by tokenizers. When the AI encounters a symbol, character, or word that is completely missing from its vocabulary, it translates it to '<UNK>' so it doesn't crash.

cards.title: The Mailbox Sorter
cards.body: Imagine a mail sorter who has cubbies for standard letters. Suddenly, a mysterious, unlabeled wooden crate arrives. Because it doesn't fit any standard cubby, the sorter slaps a giant 'UNKNOWN' sticker on it and puts it in the 'Lost & Found' bin. That bin is our <UNK> token!

cards.title: Pizza is Unknown
cards.body: Imagine your vocabulary only knows English letters. If a user types the phrase 'I love 🍕', the tokenizer reads 'I', 'love', and then sees the pizza emoji. Since it doesn't know the pizza emoji, it converts the text into: ['I', 'love', '<UNK>'].

cards.title: Myth: Unknown Characters Crash the AI
cards.body: You might think that if a tokenizer sees an unknown character, the whole system will break down and throw an error. In reality, the '<UNK>' token acts as a safety net, allowing the AI to safely ignore the mystery character and keep working.

cards.title: Known vs. Unknown
cards.body: See how the tokenizer handles words it knows versus a symbol it has never seen before.

left.text: The tokenizer finds 'Hello' in its vocabulary list and maps it to a standard token ID.

right.text: The tokenizer cannot find the emoji, so it maps it to the special '<UNK>' token instead.

exercises.prompt: If a tokenizer's vocabulary only has English words, how will it process the text 'Hello there 👾'?
exercises.explain: Since the vocabulary only contains English words, the alien emoji '👾' is completely unrecognized and gets replaced by '<UNK>'.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The '<UNK>' token is used to represent characters that are missing from the tokenizer's vocabulary.
exercises.explain: The <UNK> token is specifically designed as a placeholder for unrecognized characters so the program does not crash.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing token name that represents an unknown character.
exercises.sentence: If a tokenizer sees a symbol it doesn't know, it replaces it with the [blank] token.
exercises.explain: The <UNK> (Unknown) token is used as a placeholder for any character or symbol that isn't in the AI's vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Which statement accurately describes how a tokenizer handles an emoji it has never seen before?

options.text: AI: The tokenizer will replace the unfamiliar emoji with '<UNK>' to keep processing the text.
User A: The tokenizer will automatically add the new emoji to its permanent dictionary.
User B: The tokenizer will freeze and wait for a developer to update the code.

exercises.explain: The AI is correctly identifying that the unknown character gets swapped out for the '<UNK>' token, while the human-generated response incorrectly suggests the AI will automatically learn the new emoji on the spot.

exercises.prompt: You are building a chat app, and a user inputs a rare alien symbol that your tokenizer has never seen. What does the tokenizer do to keep the system running smoothly?

options.text: It skips the character entirely without leaving any placeholder.
It pauses the program and asks the user to type in a translation.
It replaces the character with <UNK> so the AI can still process the rest of the text.
It automatically downloads a new language pack to understand it.

exercises.explain: When a model encounters a character it has never seen before, like a rare emoji in a text-only vocabulary, it replaces it with the <UNK> token. If it tried to process the unknown character directly, the system would crash!
